title: Now
text:
What I’m working on at the moment. Last updated 16 July 2026.

Writing up three first-author manuscripts from my PhD, ahead of first submission and preprint:

The structure of developmental noise in a complete connectome
Spatial transcriptomics of the larval mushroom body: intrinsic pre-tuning of memory-circuit neurons
Towards the panconnectome



Finishing a transitional postdoc in the Cardona group at the MRC Laboratory of Molecular Biology, and moving to London in September 2026.


This is a now page; a snapshot of my current focus rather than a record.

title: Scott Wilson
text:
I am a computational biologist, who has worked across various high dimensional datasets to extract signal from noise. I recently completed my PhD in neuroscience at the University of Cambridge. Eager to pursue a career which leads to macro-level, positive outcomes across a changing world.
Before Cambridge I read Genetics at UCL and spent a research year at the Francis Crick Institute. I’m part of the Santa Fe Institute working group on representation in minds and artificial systems.
Email · GitHub

title: A first note
text:
This is a starter post so the Writing page isn’t empty. Delete this file (posts/welcome.qmd) or overwrite it with your own first piece.
To add a post, drop a new .qmd file in posts/ with a title, date, and short description in the front matter. That’s the whole workflow — write Markdown, it appears in the list.

title: Research
text:
Variability and error in a wiring diagram
Genetically identical animals, raised alike, still don’t wire identically. I measure how much brains differ from one another, and treat the mismatches — neurons that wander, double, or go missing — as evidence about how development can fail and how robust the finished circuit really is.


Where a memory physically sits
With single-cell transcriptomics I track how training reshapes gene expression in identified neurons. Massed and spaced learning, which produce short- and long-lasting memory, share no differentially expressed genes at all in mushroom-body output neurons — a hint that the molecular account of memory is not simply a slower version of the synaptic one.


From genome to circuit
I make targeted genetic changes in single, identified neurons and measure how the wiring responds, connecting what the genome specifies to what the connectome becomes.
